title: Charging a home battery on Economy 7 and discharging at peak
subtitle: Calculating whether time-of-use arbitrage makes financial sense for your system
excerpt: Economy 7 off-peak electricity at 12p/kWh versus peak rate at 27p/kWh is a 55% price difference. A 10 kWh home battery charged overnight and discharged during peak hours saves the price difference on every kWh arbitraged, minus round-trip efficiency losses. This shows the calculation and the break-even analysis.
sourceNotes: Ofgem tariff comparison data (2025/26). Battery round-trip efficiency specifications from Pylontech and CATL published data sheets. Energy Saving Trust battery storage guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A domestic rechargeable battery system that stores electricity for use at a different time from when it was generated or imported.
The ratio of energy discharged from a battery to energy used to charge it. A 90% round-trip efficiency means 10% of the energy is lost to heat during the charge/discharge cycle.

content.text: Economy 7 gives 7 hours of cheaper electricity. This is usually midnight to 7 am, but the window varies by supplier and meter type. At 2025/26 rates, the standard unit rate is around 27p/kWh and the Economy 7 off-peak rate around 12p/kWh. A
battery storage
system charged at 12p and discharged at 27p saves the 15p price difference on each arbitraged kWh, minus losses from the battery's
round-trip efficiency
(typically 90 to 95% for modern lithium systems).
The basic arbitrage calculation
For a 10 kWh battery with 90% round-trip efficiency, fully cycled daily from Economy 7 to peak:
Import 11.1 kWh at 12p/kWh to charge to 10 kWh usable (11.1 kWh because 1/0.9 = 1.11). Import cost: 133p.
Discharge 10 kWh at peak, displacing 10 kWh that would otherwise be imported at 27p. Value: 270p.
Daily saving per cycle: 270p minus 133p = 137p.
Annual saving (365 daily cycles): 137p × 365 = around 50,000p.

A 10 kWh battery system is a large capital outlay. Arbitrage saving from Economy 7 alone gives a payback of 16 to 24 years. This exceeds the battery warranty period (10 years for most lithium systems). Arbitrage alone does not make a standalone battery financially viable in 2025. The economics improve when combined with solar export timing, EV charging, and time-of-use tariffs with bigger spreads than Economy 7.
When arbitrage does make sense

Tariffs with bigger spreads than Economy 7 change the calculation. Agile Octopus and similar smart tariffs can offer off-peak rates of 5p/kWh or lower, against peak rates of 30 to 40p/kWh. At a 35p/kWh spread with 90% efficiency, the same 10 kWh cycle saves around 311p per day. This gives a payback of 7 to 11 years, within the battery warranty period on a well-managed system.

The strongest case is a household already on Economy 7 for storage heaters or an immersion heater at night. Adding a battery and using the arbitrage on top cuts the payback period. No extra standing charge, because you are already on the tariff.
Practical setup
In the battery management system (BMS) or inverter schedule, set the charge window to match your Economy 7 window. Most hybrid inverters (Solaredge, GivEnergy, Victron) have a timed charge schedule.

Set the discharge start time to after the Economy 7 window ends. Do not allow the battery to discharge back to the grid during the off-peak window (the SEG export rate is 5 to 15p/kWh, lower than the off-peak import rate).
Set a reserve: keep 10 to 20% state of charge as a reserve for outages. Fully cycling to 0% every day degrades lithium batteries faster.

Monitor after the first week: compare the import meter reading during the Economy 7 window with the discharge meter reading in peak hours. If the battery is not completing a full charge in the 7-hour window, the charge current limit may need adjusting.
Common variations

Solar + battery: if the system also has solar, the charge schedule prioritises solar charging during the day. Economy 7 charging fills the remaining capacity overnight. Arbitrage saving is lower because some battery capacity is reserved for solar.

Three-phase supply: most domestic battery systems are single-phase. A three-phase house needs a three-phase compatible battery (rarer, more expensive) or accepts that the battery only offsets one phase.
